narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown real determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60, which stands out above the typical student's 3.21. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your risk of failure at 66.8%—a threshold that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower decile rankings in school grades from Year 1 and especially Year 3, both at the 1st decile compared to averages around the 5th or 6th, which strongly signaled to the model a higher chance of bar exam struggles. These early academic placements had the biggest influence, pushing the prediction toward failure more than any other factor. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, countering that risk by suggesting stronger performance potential than most applicants. Your lower-middle family income bracket also nudged things slightly toward success, as did your LSAT score of 31, though its impact was minimal, and your full-time student status had almost no effect either way. Compared to typical students, your high school deciles are notably lower, while your college grades exceed the norm, highlighting areas where you've grown but also where the model sees persistent early challenges. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers in law—it's just the model's data-driven assessment. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted tutoring to strengthen your academic decile equivalents in future applications, or exploring programs with holistic reviews that value your unique background. Many students in similar situations improve their profiles and succeed—keep pushing, and we believe in your ability to overcome this. Reach out to admissions counselors for personalized advice on reapplying stronger.
